ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Afghani Mutton Karhai; closest match is lamb dish food product. Mutton and lamb are used interchangeably in South Asian culinary context.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers cooked sheep meat as the closest defensible category; no specific Afghani preparation code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Afghani Mutton dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Afghani Mutton is approximately 250 g, comprising bone-in or boneless mutton pieces in semi-dry spiced gravy, usually served alongside naan or rice.

nutrient_source.title: Afghani Mutton — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95 with review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken directly from the HPB Foodo entry for Afghani Mutton (cr_id G00010931). Saturated fat is estimated at approximately 5.5 g/100g based on typical mutton-and-cream composition; monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat, and detailed micronutrient fractions not reported in HPB entry and set to -1.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Meat and Spice Dishes — Analogous Estimate Based on Low-Carbohydrate Meat Curry
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Afghani Mutton Karhai in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous meat-based curry dishes with moderate carbohydrate content (7.44 g/100g) from tomatoes and onions. High protein (14.05 g/100g) and fat (11.58 g/100g) content significantly attenuate postprandial glucose response. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as: (35 × 7.44 × 2.5) / 100 = 6.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 467 mg/100g with low dietary fibre (0.3 g/100g) and elevated saturated fat (~5.5 g/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150 g, request reduced-salt and reduced-cream preparation, and pair with high-fibre, high-potassium sides such as lentils or spinach to partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate (7.44 g/100g) and sugar (4.69 g/100g) with high protein (14.05 g/100g) attenuating glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Afghani Mutton has a low estimated GI (~35) and moderate carbohydrate content; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should pair it with low-GI carbohydrates such as basmati rice or wholegrain roti rather than white naan, and monitor portion size due to the moderate saturated fat content which may worsen insulin resistance with frequent consumption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat ~5.5 g/100g from mutton and cream, cholesterol 85 mg/100g, and sodium 467 mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Afghani Mutton only occasionally, request reduced cream and leaner mutton cuts, limit portions to 100–150 g, and avoid pairing with high-sodium condiments, given the combined saturated fat, cholesterol, and sodium burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Mutton is red meat high in arachidonic acid (omega-6 pro-inflammatory pathway) and saturated fat ~5.5 g/100g exceeds the 5 g/100g threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit Afghani Mutton due to the arachidonic acid content of red meat which amplifies inflammatory pathways; substituting mutton with chicken or fish and requesting reduced cream would substantially lower the inflammatory burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Afghani Mutton is a South Asian meat dish prepared in the Afghani karhai style, featuring bone-in or boneless mutton pieces cooked in a semi-dry spiced gravy. The dish is characterised by bold use of black pepper, coriander, and cumin, with tomatoes and onions forming the gravy base, and cream added for richness. It is commonly served in Indian and Pakistani restaurants in Singapore and across the South Asian diaspora. Per 100 g, it provides 192 kcal, 14.05 g protein, 11.58 g fat (approximately 5.5 g saturated), and 7.44 g carbohydrate, with 467 mg sodium and 85 mg cholesterol. The high protein content supports satiety and slows gastric emptying, giving the dish a low estimated glycaemic index of approximately 35. Clinically, the elevated sodium and saturated fat make it a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients, while the moderate carbohydrate load is manageable for Type 2 Diabetes when paired with low-GI accompaniments. The red meat base is a hard disqualifier for rheumatoid arthritis dietary management. The dish is halal and gluten-free but contains dairy from cream.
common_user_queries: Is Afghani Mutton good for diabetics?
Can I eat Afghani Mutton with high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Input record sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010931) with review_status reviewed and confidence 0.95. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present from the primary regional source. Saturated fat is estimated from typical mutton-and-cream composition as HPB does not separately report fatty acid fractions for this dish.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin profile, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for Afghani Mutton are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (G00010931) as of 2026-04-16. saturated_fat_g is estimated at 5.5 g/100g based on typical composition of mutton (bone-in, cooked) and cream as reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked lamb/mutton and cream; this is an approximation and should be replaced with a direct HPB-reported value when available. GI value of 35 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database meat curry analogues; no direct GI measurement for Afghani Mutton Karhai exists in the approved GI database. Multilingual local names (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are provided as this dish is served in Singapore and classified under Indian cuisine per the input cultural tags. The dish is classified as Indian cuisine with Singaporean regional context (region_tags: SG).
